commentBody: It doesn’t have to ask me if I’m sure, and remind me I will loose my strike EVERYTIME i want so see a Word or a square. Especially in all the crosswords from 2025…

"Can I have some of your fruit juice?"
"You're always asking! This is the last straw!"

(At least I think that was the punch line.)

To me, madly funny and madly easy. Great theme and great theme execution from Rich Katz. Editors, could you please toughen up the fill clues a couple of notches? I don't think most solvers will need crosses to get the themers. Thanks.
Great! Loved the puzzle, fill was just right. One of the box sets happened to be my family members' names, a jarring surprise!

I was going to kvetch that if we’re scraping the bottom of the vocabulary barrel with the likes of IMAGO, BRAE, NOTONABET, and SAIDSHH, there’d better be a theme or stunt that justifies all that glue, but honestly the wordplay with DOWNINTHEDUMPS solely makes it up to me.

Very fun! I worked bottom up chuckled when I figured out the first themed answer (113A) and tried to solve the others with a few crosses. My only hangup was holding onto WARWORN for too long, scratching my head trying to think of a poet with that would fit. Felt like the second coming for me.

{Chorus, orange, finish}




COLOR INSIDE THE LINES
Northeast corner was a bit tricky but the rest was fun! Finished 10 minutes ahead of my Saturday average.
Enjoyed the set theme, especially TWOBIRDSWITHONESTONE and HONORAMONGTHIEVES.
Enjoyable puzzle. The themes made me smile.
This was fantastic!  Each theme answer brought a laugh.  Thank you for such a clever and rewarding puzzle.

I just read Rich Katz's comments.  I love PLAYS WITH MATCHES.  That would have been great.  With respect to the question you asked about the elegance, or lack thereof, of the grid, it didn't affect me at all.  Your puzzle was a complete delight.  Take a bow!
Loved it! Favorite is Beats Around The Bush

Thoroughly enjoyed this clever theme and appreciated the fun of trying to figure these out on my own. Was trying to fit in something related to ELECTRIC (WC) FIELDS.
Reminded me of Sesame Street: “One of these things is not like the other…”
Interesting that KABALA gets a spelling warning today and CABALA didn’t the other day.
CBGB—remembered it from the other day—he CAN be taught!
Love the clue for ODE!
The top quarter started a little crunchy for me but then as it progressed was pretty straightforward. Still adored the theme and execution.

Personal best!

I noticed the word "beats" occurs in the clues and in the grid. I thought that was against the rules. Maybe it's more of a guideline.

I can never remember if it goes {Buck, Bailey, Wilbur} or {Wilbur, Buck, Bailey}.
Either way, not as funny as ROLL WITH THE PUNCHES!
@Caitlin. It was only Thursday when these comment were flooded with complaints that the (only) correct spelling was “KABBALAH9, or some other form beginning with K. “CAB…” was definitely “wrong”.

Quite clever and entertaining, but also over too quickly. I could have used a little more of a challenge on a Sunday puzzle.

Thanks, Rich.

pretty fast and pretty fun. themers were a hoot, and rest of fill didn't do anything to annoy me--perhaps because i got a lot from crosses and therefore may have missed several clues. Got a kick out of ENDCAPS; ever since I learned they had a name I've found it useful. I noticed KABALA came with a slight caveat; I wonder if that was edited last minute in response to the recent brouhaha. And I wonder if CBGB will be remembered by those who encountered it so recently. This ain't no disco. I feel like I've encountered OBEAH before, but now as then i got it entirely from crosses. 

Stats says I solved it in 50% of my Sunday average, but the timer on the puzzle puts it at a few minutes longer than my average, and that feels about right. I do wish the IT team could hunt down that bug and fix it. 

carry on.

The was the most fun you could hope for in a Sunday puzzle.  I filled in entries quickly so that I could get to the next delightfully punny trio.  Hard to pick a favorite, but 97A was a winner.  

A couple of clues I needed help with and had to come to the column to get (ESPORT crossing ESPNU stopped me in my tracks, and 75 both ways) but all the other squares were fair.

I loved your construction, Mr. Katz, and was not even remotely bothered by the black spots in the corners—they sort of pinned it to the wall.  Thank you, and let us have the next one soon.

@riled 
Yesterday I replied to a reply and my reply was sort of buried. I saw your question today about how long I have been working the Times crosswords, and this was my reply:
 
Crosswords have been a daily habit for ±70 years, and I have myriad memories associated with working them—mornings on a San Francisco cable car on my way to work, at Paris sidewalk cafes, in the International Herald Tribune, on my iPad in a hospital, and more than I can remember. As with so many others, I trust that it will be accessible wherever I am, because it grounds me.  By this time, it is part of who I am.

My new passion in the Spelling Bee: 1,070 puzzles, 35,358 words,1,065 Geniuses, 1,368 pangrams, only 11 Queen Bees, but I'm still trying for more. I'm sure many others have far better totals than I do and love dredging up words you never knew you knew as much as I do.

(Sorry, Crossword, I never meant to be unfaithful.)
This is my FUN place. I don’t want politics.

Fun Sunday today, though easy by Sunday standards.  It might be my PR, but I'm not sure because my PR is incorrectly set to something like 4 minutes.

The theme was pretty fun to discover.  The first one I got was BABE IN THE WOODS, but once I had that one I knew exactly what to look for with the others.  Funny.

A few answers around I still am not quite sure what they mean but all of those had good crossings.  I struggled at the end putting in ODESSEY instead of ODYSSEY because I know nothing about ELY.

A few others like REB, LETT, etc I have no idea what the answers refer to, but nothing in that category that didn't have good crossings.
NBA refs as enforcers of traveling restrictions? Sadly, not quite accurate these days!

Another fun one! Lots of chuckles! 

Once I got ROLLWITHTHEPUNCHES, the nickel dropped and I went back up and got most of them with immediacy. I struggled to understand BEATSAROUNDTHEBUSH because I didn't get the BEATS part, but I was SELF confident in my crosses. I do get it now, though, from the column.  

Hard to pick a favorite themer, but POWERBEHINDTHETHRONE made me laugh the hardest. I fear a 13 year old boy resides somewhere deep within me just for moments like these....  I won't get DOWNINTHEDUMPS about it, but still, GODHELPME! 

I've even been sleeping in like a teen these last few days! Hope I'm not going through a growth spurt! I don't need to go up or out!! Har!  

But seriously, wow, this has been such a long and slothful weekend, other than puzzles, and uh, a major holiday, and my husband just reminding me that my birthday is on Thursday, I seriously have no concept of time.  I keep thinking tomorrow is Monday when my slothfulness must come to an end and I have to get a GRIP, but so far it hasn't been Monday since last Monday. Isn't that wild!? 

Cheers to one more day of weekend!!

Well, didn’t this just fit the bill perfectly.  

It’s a holiday, I don’t want to grind too hard.  I didn’t.  Yet my brain got its quota of exercise.

It’s a holiday, I want to have fun, and I did as I guessed at the theme answers from their clues. I want to be kept in a good mood, and some of those answers made me smile, or even go “Hah!”.

This was not heavy and cumbersome.  It was a guy with a good sense of humor throwing it into the box and saying, “Have a good time!”

And I did.  Your puzzle, Rich, simply hit the spot. Oh, I would have loved it any Sunday, but I especially loved it today.  Thank you!

If you are up for another crossword, and want to see what nonsense my brain can come up with, the LA Times is publishing my Sunday puzzle this weekend as a special birthday present for me. I think they post midnight EST.
Unlike the NYT we don’t get to review the final version, so I can’t make any guarantees about the overall challenge or any individual clues. They did do a very patient, meticulous job working with me on the theme entries and grid, so I’m sure their editing will be wonderful.
If you do try it and want to comment, just remember to include a spoiler alert before any specific comments or notes.

Smart puzzle! Much better than the others, or...

CRANIUM
DELTOIDS
CAESURA

Very clever and fun! Thanks, Rich. 

Nice touch with UNO, DOS and TRES. 

[Bronze, Iron, Pulchritude]

AGE BEFORE BEAUTY

Glad I learned about CBGB eight days ago! It went from "never heard of it" to gimme.

Another curious coincidence was that we had CABALA a couple days ago; some (not me!) were griping about the spelling. I entered it confidently today, but it turned out to be the wrong spelling this time. (Thankfully, the crosser, KEY, was straightforward.)

I agree with the constructor that the grid design might not be the most elegant, but that's forgivable in my view. I suspect that most solvers don't really care all that much about that aspect of construction.

{Today’s crossword, bees, intercom}

Buzzing after this!

Terrific! 
I had such a great time figuring out the theme entries, avoiding as much as possible the crosses. 

And the fill was gentle and mild, perfect for a holiday weekend.
Thank you so much, Mr. Katz!

As for the grid design you inquire about, please know that there was nothing *wrong* with it. 
Sometimes a beautiful design does jump out, but that doesn't mean that a non-beautiful one elicits any dislike. 

The puzzle itself was so engaging that had you not brought up the presence of a black square at the start, I wouldn't have noticed it.

This was a fabulous ROMP!
Enjoyed it! All except for that KABALA crossing OBEAH part.

I'm about to write to tech support but would like to know if others have encountered the same glitch:

(On Android, in the Games app)
I'm having to do a "check puzzle" every so often, just to ensure my entries get locked in.

If I don't do that and leave the app to, for example, answer a message, when I return to the app most of what I've entered disappears! 

It's been happening for a few days now.

I don't worry about streaks but I do like my gold stars, so this glitch is annoying me because the solution automatically blue stars the puzzle.

Anyone else experiencing this?

Super easy puzzle, cute theme, but I stopped by entirely to praise the brilliance of "What has flatter feet?"

A terrific, innovative way to clue one of the crustiest and commonest of crosswordese.  Loved it.

Early results show this puzzle to be Ridiculously...er, Very Easy:

🌎  Global Stats
Difficulty
Very Easy
Median Solve Time
20:11
Median Solver
29% faster
⚡92% of users solved faster than their Sunday average.
68% solved much faster (>20%) than their Sunday average.
🐢8% of users solved slower than their Sunday average.
2% solved much slower (>20%) than their Sunday average.

Geography and NYC culture always easy for me. I've been to the ODEON, and I'm actually going to a restaurant on the NYT 100 Best Restaurants list called ACCRA Express this week. Easy 18 minute solve. Liked the theme
I think I'm getting some kind of crossword PTSD --- I kept looking for a gimmick. What I got was a very good Sunday puzzle. Thanks!
Not difficult once I got 41 across, but really clever and fun.

Lightning very nearly struck twice.

Last night I foundered on the TARANA/SCHEMA cross.

I *finally* saw that ODeSSEY/Ele was not right.  But it took me forever to remember how to spell "odyssey".  That really, really looks wrong to me, still.

Moreover, I had a lot of other worrisome spots.  I'd never heard of   ESPORT, OBEAH, IMAGO, TRENTA, OLGA...I relied almost entirely on crosses for those.

Thankfully we saw KABALA (in slightly different form) and CGBG a couple of nights ago.

All in all, though, I thought this was a terrific puzzle.

So I'm back to all chillaxed.  Maybe I won't be so obnoxious in the forum tonight.

Breezy Sunday. I LOVED the theme!!! 

Rating:
9/10
A brilliant erudite masterwork!  No tricks. No gimmicks. No circles. Just a fabulous use of the mother tongue!  👏👏👏👏👏👏👏

BEVY is such a lovely word.
Rich, my favorite themer was the one you couldn't use, but of the ones that made it into the puzzle, I liked ROLL WITH THE PUNCHES the most, though all were good.
Thanks.
Fun puzzle.  Asthetically pleasing enough to me, as it was symmetrical kitty corner to kitty corner.
Great fun, 🤩 lots of laughs with the theme entries. 41A also my fave! Still chuckling. Happy 4th.

The past couple years, I’ve pretty much only been a Friday and Saturday solver and very sporadically at that. For whatever reason, I decided this week to start doing the puzzle every day, and it turns out based on another commenter that this week’s early week puzzles were better than average, so I got lucky. 

Then For Saturday’s puzzle, I was actually the first commenter. Not that that was my ambition, but it was the first time I’ve ever been even close to one of the first comments.

Now for Sunday, I actually got A new personal best. It was only 24 minutes, but for me Sundays on a phone are torture and I usually don’t even try to  it. When I do, even if I’m flying, it’s usually 40 minutes trying to zoom in and out of this 21 x 21 grid.  I think 24 minutes on a phone is equivalent to 15 minutes on a Laptop.

Anyway, I don’t know what’s going on this week. Maybe it’s the crossword gods Rewarding me for trying to do the puzzle every day again. But I know I’ll never do a Sunday as Quickly as I did today. And I don’t ‘rush’. When I solve because I don’t really care about times or streaks   

But not gonna lie, NYT crosswords making me feel pretty good about myself this week
Ask me if you need help on 107 across. 😃
After watching the NBA finals last month, it is pretty obvious that they no longer enforce traveling 😂.

Enjoyed the theme, but the creator tried to get a bit too clever with some other clues though.

There was a fair amount of me rolling my eyes and saying, "Really!?" when answering some of the more inane ones. 14 down was of particular annoyance! 

I feel its the editors who need to up their game though. It's difficult to create a great puzzle without some quality oversight, and it feels like that that has been lacking for a while now.

Not a fan of this puzzle at all.
First, too easy for a Sunday, and finished in half my average time. But more importantly, the themers were inconsistent. Elijah and Natalie are WOODS, but BABE is a Ruth i.e. The inconsistency of putting two first names with a last name. Likewise the Dubya clue. Finally, kabala crossing obeah is a massive Natick because, as Caitlin points out, kabala can be spelled so many different ways. If we just spell things the way we want, why do crosswords at all??

And before Barry jumps in and says "you could get it from the crosses", I had Keats before YEATS, so spelling CABALA yielded CEK. That whole section was a Natick. Crosswords are dependent on correct spelling, so if a word can be spelled 10 different ways, either don't include it, or don't cross it with 2 other proper nouns. (and no, Kabala was not crossed with yeats, but in this section CABALA, Keats and obeah was a huge Natick.)
I really enjoyed this puzzle! It made me smile, and I thought it was clever! I didn’t mind the easy clues at all. Who says obscure is the criterion for a good puzzle!

I just loved this theme.  Each one was perfect, but if I had to pick one and only one, I'd go with HONOR AMONG THIEVES,
or DOWN IN THE DUMPS,
or BEATS AROUND THE BUSH,
or...  
See?  I can't even pick a favorite.

I only wish the cluing was toughened up for the non-themers so I could have spent more time with it.

KABALA… CBGB… THIS is why I call it learning, not cheating! (Although to get today’s spelling of “kabala”, you’d have had to read the daily comments. Also not a cheat.)

How many other solvers did today’s puzzle on the floor, one-handed, while stroking a dog’s trembling face and body with the other? I used to love the 4th of July and fireworks shows. Now I just want to track down the neighbors who shoot off bottle rockets illegally and shove one up their emu. You know what I mean.
A very enjoyable Sunday puzzle, extending my streak to 700 😁

[Romeo Rip Juliet]

Torn between two lovers

Truly enjoyed it, Mr. Katz.

In response to your question-

I open the puzzle and 
(always in this order)
take in the symmetry and 
subsequent vibe of the grid.

Then I count the rows.

Today,
I saw an odd smattering of black
in what (if I recall correctly?) was
diagonal symmetry.

An extra row.

And then the black squares
in the NW and SE corners.

I agree that it was not pretty,

but I was so intrigued by what theme
would cause you to require such a grid,
and excited to think that the editors
agreed it was worthy of my
impending solve!

It certainly was.

My mother would 
brush and braid my hair 
when I was young,
and when I’d wince

she say in Swedish
 (it rhymes in Swedish)

“If you want to be beautiful
you must suffer a little pain.”


Your puzzle was beautiful,
even if the grid was a wee bit painful.


Thank you!
Delightful! Got stuck by the Natick, though.

I guess the constructor and I have different definitions of a 'powerhouse', because it's not a word I'd usually choose to describe a team with only a single (and now defunct!) major European trophy in its history...

That aside, great puzzle!
I enjoyed that one

[What has flatter feet?] is a terrific clue for ODE, and a memory trigger for me. At my exit (DEROS/ETS) physical at Fort Dix in 1971, the orthopod examined me and said "You have flat feet; what were you doing in the Army?" I asked him where he'd been at my pre-induction physical.

Needed a little assist to get the theme; I got the first one without really understanding the BABE part, so thanks Caitlin for explaining. 
The rest was fun, despite the knotty (to me) unknown acronyms crossing place names. 
Funny to see CBGB again so quickly, plus KABALA. I know the first from my punk days, the second only through Madonna trying to get then husband Guy Richie into it. Funny what trivia sticks in the mind. I think it’s because LaDonna tried to get our then local airfield closed down as she felt it interfered with her private helicopter. You move next to WWII airfield, you accept there will be other planes sweetie. Though I think the planning dept put it a little more politely.
It's a preposition party!! I especially loved DOWN IN THE DUMPS. This was creative and fun!!

{Kerouac, Dubya, Ginsberg} for BEATSAROUNDTHEBUSH was my favorite by far.

{WC, AC, DC} for POWERBEHINDTHETHRONE struck me as desperation on the part of the constructor.

Enjoyed this about as much as usual for a Sunday.  ;-)

Two nits that perhaps only the NSA folks will appreciate:

(1) The clue for 121A really should have been "Thousands of zeros and ones".  As given, it comes very close to breaking a very important rule, since "bits" is a portmanteau of "binary digits".

(2) The theme depends on the *ordering* of the entries in the triads, e.g. 41A. Sets are unordered, so the use of braces as delimiters was inapt, as was the puzzle's title.

Yes, I'm loads of fun at parties.

This puzzle wasn’t easy, which is why  it was such a lot of fun. I find with puzzles as clever as this, thinking actually interferes with knowing   The themes were readily accessible because I  just let them float into place from deep familiarity with our  language’s idioms and stock phrases.
I should have gotten "two birds with one stone more quickly than I did, since I taught both Larry and Sue's two children.
This one fell into place rather quickly once I figured out the theme. Solved before I came here to look at the tricky clues in 24:13. Not a personal best, but close.
With the dumb dad-joke themes, this should have been a father's day puzzle!

Started chewing on this last evening and finished this morning. Great fun (or romp as we say). Loved figuring out each theme clue. Like an old fashioned REBUS puzzle that they would put in the old Sunday Parade magazine. Favorite was Two Birds With One Stone. Puzzle design looks fine to me. Would rather a black square than an  esoteric clue picked to fit! It "firmed up" the puzzle nicely 😊.

You could say that the theme was too easy to figure out this morning (I'd say it but it would sound like complaining and I don't like to complain, especially about well-crafted puzzles like this one).  Well done, Rich!

Nice easy, breezy Sunday. I usually start Sunday puzzles by filling in (on paper) whatever clue jumps out at me first. Since I'm short and work on the kitchen counter, it's usually toward the bottom. And today's was no exception. Immediately got the last themer, saw where the trick was going, then went right up to the top and got the first themer. It was downhill, literally, from there.

Was pleased to see some old-timey crosswordese, like OBEAH, LETT, and BRAE, a repeat of some very recent ones, like CBGB, KABALA and UNO, and themers that mostly made me smile. 

Thanks, Rich.
Great fun!  Once I got 41A (also my favorite) I was chuckling the rest of the way.  And I was glad to see two hoops-related answers.

Like @Vaer, ROLLWITHTHEPUNCHES was my favorite.  It was my first theme answer and made me realize the theme.

Rich, thank you for a fun ROMP.  And I’m agnostic about the northwest corner.
A fun Sunday puzzle.  Once I got the device it was a nice challenge deducing the theme fills.  The NE gave me fits, however.
Nice, challenging-enough puzzle. Happy Independence from boredom Day!

Wonderful Sunday puzzle. Excellent theme and enjoyed every themed answer. Each time I asked myself, "What do these three have to do with one another?" Only to go "D'oh!" and give myself a dope-slap a few minutes later when the answer became clear. (After I completed 66-Across, I wondered, "Who is this 'Oscar HONOR'? I don't think I've ever heard of him." And then the penny dropped. Another dope slap. That's the kind of self-abuse I enjoy these days. Favorite? I'd say it was a tie between BEATS AROUND THE BUSH and TWO BIRDS WITH ONE STONE. As someone who avoids Starbucks on principle, I had trouble with the 75-Across. I had _R_N__, and confidently put in gRaNde, and had to pull out the eraser when the "Tesla bumper sticker" clue appeared. Even after that the crossing of 75-A and 75-D was the last letter to be filled. _ATS had many possible answers to the intentionally enigmatic clue. GATS and GRENTA, perhaps? And TRENTA? If you're getting a 30-ounce coffee, you've got a serious caffeine issue. I suggest you get help. "Venti" should be more than enough! But the "Tall, Grande, Venti" issue reminded me of this funny scene with Paul Rudd. "Congratulations! You're stupid in three languages." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tbb7a404i78
My copy of the magazine was missing the puzzle pages.  Any way to get a replacement?  Or at least to print the puzzle?
What a fun puzzle! Thanks, Rich.
I loved this and I'm glad everyone else did too! N.B.: I haven't read any of the comments yet.

I enjoyed this quite a bit. Took me a bit to find my way in but once I did I solved fairly quickly (around 40% under my average time. I loved the theme..honestly, every theme clue tickled my funny bone.
I loved this puzzle. The theme answers made me laugh.
That was so much fun! I agree that the look of the grid is a little off-putting, but the theme more than makes up for it.
Just plain awesome. Thanks!!

DOWN IN THE DUMPS clued me in, and after that I twisted my brain AROUND to get the other tricks.  The only one that is still baffling is the one at 22A; I see BABE Ruth, but I have no idea who the other WOODS are.  And Sue BIRD? Who?
 I may look them up now that DHubby has topped up my coffee.

At least this round I knew CBGB (or, I got it, but I still have no real idea about what where why when.)  ERHU is my new word to look up.  
(Who actually SHOUTS "Uno" in the card game? Pfft.)

I had to run the alphabet for TATS.  
HOMIE?  Isn't it HOMEY?  
OLGA...Slavic?  Common in Scandinavian/Russian literature; not quite the same thing?

We managed to Not Watch the July 4th TV coverage (many of which were cancelled by the Weather Gods) but we did view "The Best Years of Our Lives"...  and enjoyed the Cincinnati scenes (Lunkin Field, Walnut Hills High School, the Viaduct, etc.)  Harold Russell lived to the age of 88.  His autobiographical _Victory in my Hands_ is a worthwhile read; 9th grade Lit text had a portion of that book included.  (Some of our teachers were WWII veterans ...)

Today's earworm, courtesy of Cole Porter and 85-Across [and Ella Fitzgerald https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lXYKGL6MgKM] And that's why birds do it, bees do it, Even educated fleas do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love In Spain, the best upper sets do it; Lithuanians and LETTs do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love The Dutch in old Amsterdam do it Not to mention the Finns Folks in Siam do it -- think of Siamese twins Some Argentines without means do it; People say in Boston even beans do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love Romantic sponges, they say, do it; Oysters down in Oyster Bay do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love Cold Cape Cod clams, 'gainst their wish, do it Even lazy jellyfish, do it Let's do it, let's fall in love Electric eels I might add do it, 'Though it shocks 'em I know Why ask if shad do it? Waiter bring me shad roe In shallow shoals, English soles do it; Goldfish in the privacy of bowls do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love. The dragonflies in the reeds do it; Sentimental centipedes do it. Let's do it it, let's fall in love. Mosquitoes, heaven forbids, do it; So does every katydid do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love The most refined ladybugs do it, When a gentleman calls. Moths in your rugs do it. What's the use of moth balls? Locusts in trees do it; bees do it; even over-educated fleas do it. Let's do it, let's fall in love!

Really enjoyed this one. Typical long workout for me, but just had a lot of fun getting enough crosses for each of the theme answers to dawn on me. Puzzle find today is a Sunday from July 26, 2020 by Trenton Charlson with the title "Made-To-Order". I'm sure many of us had done this one, but I had completely forgotten it. I'll just list some theme answers with the 'trick' section highlighted. "Facial feature of a Lego man? squareCHIN Some others: HOSTinthedark ACESdismissed ABETroundthebush BELOWmacaroni And there were others. Here's that link; https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/26/2020&h=67a ....
I love when I have to "check" on a puzzle, but I still derive enjoyment from seeing what I missed and how the puzzle comes together. Very fun and enjoyable.

Superb puns and wordplay!  Great cluing choices!  I had a wonderful time with the theme answers.  As always, I tried to get them with as few crosses as possible.  In almost every case I got them from just the first word.  In TWO BIRDS WITH ONE STONE, however, I had the BIRDS but not the TWO, and got hung up on the expression "birds of a feather" -- so I didn't see it as early as I should have.

All the puns are perfection -- my faves being DOWN IN THE DUMPS and HONOR AMONG THIEVES.  As for the rest of the puzzle: it was the admission price for getting to guess at the themers.   Being the hedonist that I am, I skipped filling in the surrounding fill just as soon as I had the info I needed.  On a long Sunday puzzle, the clues of the surrounding fill need to inspire curiosity for me to want to bother with them, and these didn't especially for me.  But the theme is absolutely delightful.

What a really wonderful puzzle week this has been!!

well today is obviously not the day I finally learn to spell 124A. Had to flyspeck hard. 171D was no help.
  otherwise cluing was ATAD too easy...and the spanners were cute but obvious.
Whoo, that went quickly

Belatedly catching up and still haven't done Sunday's puzzle. But I do want to wish the nation to the south a happy 250th (+1 day). 

And for those of you who wish that this bloated rutabaga of a year wasn't your 250th: this is an artifact of your nation's adherence to the iron hand of decimalism. Be creative—go OCTAL! In an eight-based number system, you turned 250 decades—oops! I mean octades ago.

Yes, in an octal numbering system, 250 years (base 8) = 2 x 64 + 5 x 8 = 168 (base 10) years. In octal, your 250th was 1776+168 years = 1944. 
(Sadly, I think going octal for the /numbering of years/ won't catch on, but just as you hang on to British imperial measures, you could surely impose octal calculations for historic spans.)  

1944! Now that was a US worth fighting for and the proof is that you fought for it.

Your 350th (base 8) arrived after 3 x 64 +5 x 8 = 232 (base 10) years, i.e., 2008. A stinker of a year economically but imagine how your nation's spirit could have been buoyed by the recollection (currently forbidden) of your glorious and exemplary struggles to become a better nation for all your inhabitants.

Your 400th will occur 4 × 64 = 256 years after your founding, i.e. in 2032. (You'll notice that, in base 8, these landmarks just zoom right up.)  Start planning your celebration today. Happy 400th (oct) birthday, USA! I'm looking forward to coming down and joining in.
Why is the picture of some dude in a marijuana farm?

Got the theme as soon as I was DOWN IN THE DUMPS. 

Regarding kilobits for speed, I've never quite understood why they are preferable to kilobytes. To reduce the confusion between measures of speed and storage? Pah. Along with @SBK in TO's suggestion below to switch to OCTAL, I vote we switch from kbps to KBps.
“Tonne” is a unit of mass not weight, those aren’t the same thing

I really disliked the Babe in the woods clue.

Elijah, Ruth, and Natilie all are people with the surname of Wood related to acting, with Ruth Wood being Clint Eastwood's mother.

Switching from surnames to a first name in the middle and to a baseball player and not actor.

A more appropriate clue would have been Elijah, Fabienne, Natalie.

Well that was fun! Very clever.

I couldn't resist trying my hand at one.

(Cues, Hues, Queues)

colors inside the lines

I'm surprised that nobody complained about the ERHU. Is Chinese opera blowing up on the socials? I always complain about them, because they're so shrill. Fun theme, not too much of a challenge.

I recently learned that the proper name of  65D was the Santa Clara, and that "Little Girl" was a nickname the sailors gave her.
For the longest time I had BEYOND THE THRONE.
Absolutely charming...what great Sundaty chuckles. Bravo, Rich!
Loved it! Fun and no complaints.
Loved the theme! Small nit - shouldn’t 41A parse to “POWERS BEHIND THE THRONE”? I know that doesn’t fit the grid, but seemed odd to me after parsing WOODS, etc.
Clever theme and really fun solve! Happy camper on this fine Sunday.

Not my best Sunday time but my best time on Sunday! Echoing others but that was the most fun I’ve had on a Sunday puzzle. Once in a while it’s nice to have a puzzle filled with-in from left to right, top to bottom, NE to SW without much of a pause…and it’s rare I figure out the spanners so quickly.

I can't believe my time on this one...11 mins faster than my Sunday average!!!! What a rip roaring fun time!! Genuinely loved all of the theme clues. Kind of hate that the second I read 24A, the answer came to me immediately. Too corporatepilled! Fantastic work, Mr. Katz. Come back soon!

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from July 8, 2018 by Bruce Haight with the title "Person/Place/Thing." Thought this was pretty amazing. Some theme answers: ALGREENBAYWINDOW PARISHILTONHEADSHOP OLIVERNORTHPOLEDANCE SEANPENNSTATIONBREAK RICHLITTLEROCKMUSIC MAEWESTBANKHEIST Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/8/2018&h=91a ...
Dutchiris, your inspiration pushed me to try to go beyond mere genius level—I kept it going a bit longer, and guess what happened today?  Queen Bee, unaided!! TY!

(Cirrus, Edith, cumulus)  --  Head in the clouds

(Padre, spit, double)  -- Takes after his father

Started off struggling until my first aha moment.
That was followed by bunches of feel-good aha moments.
Thanks for creating this one!
Fun puzzle, but I have to say I've never heard of the phrase NOT ON A BET. Might be a generational thing.

And... a puzzle find. The strangest I've ever encountered. I have to confess that I do not grasp the theme of this one at all. Anyway - a Sunday from July 30, 1989 by Frances Hansen with the title: "How's that again?" Some theme answers in order: IDOUBTIDDEEMITADISGRACE WEREYOUTOCALLAVASEAVASE INDEEDIDNOTBEGIVENPAUSE WEREYOUTOCALLAVASEAVASE IMVERYTOLERANTTHESEDAYS ITSNOTSOTOUGHIHAFMYVASE I feel like I just must be missing something there. I don't get it at all. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/30/1989&h=64a ....

The answer to 112 across is incorrect: it confuses the verbs "lie" and "lay".  Inexcusable in the Times, which should set a standard.
Further, although admittedly more esoteric, the structure of the themed clues shows an ignorance of mathematical terminology.  In a set the order in which the elements are written down is irrelevant; for example, {WC,AC,DC} is just the same as {AC,DC,WC}.  But clearly the order plays a role in the clues.

Saying that a "gamer's pursuit" is "esports" feels dated considering the bottom fell out of esports a half a decade ago and has really been on a downward trend ever since. Maybe the clue should have been "dying pastime of some gamers."
I have such admiration for crossword developers; this was a beauty.  The clue them was very fun.  By the time I got to the last one, it was a slam dunk!  Thank you, Rich Katz!
Loved this puzzle!  I got 29D as for Shel Silverstein, as he lived in Key West, and thus might serve as the conchologist.
Loved this!

I haven't commented in ages, and my thoughts on this one probably duplicate a lot of others, but I wanted to say that I really enjoyed it, especially trying to figure out what the three components of the boxed set were going to refer to before the crosses gave me assistance. Nice job!

The greatest of fun. I got those amazing long ones without too much difficulty, it was the small very American clues that I had to look up. 
One puzzled me - there is a special word for a couple spending time together…. US TIME? when I was part of a couple that was usually 5 nights a week, it was normal time. No?

After 22A "Babes in the Woods" I kept thinking "I don't remember any Natalie in the Bible.  Nor orphans for that matter."  It took 28A "Beats around the Bush" for my eureka.  Ginsberg makes me howl anyway.

Kerouac makes me lay rubber.

Thank you Rich Katz for a delightful Sunday puzzle.
I hope all my fellow Americans have had a wonderful safe weekend. Happy Birthday to US!

This is one of the most creative and interesting themes I've encountered in the Sunday puzzle in years!  I particularly enjoyed it because I studied set theory as a math major in college.  I hope to see many more puzzles by Mr. Katz in the future, but he'll have to go a long way to top this one!
Fun one! Got the clue quickly and finished in just 30 secs over my PB. Wordle today is a different story …
Cute theme and fun puzzle. The clue for 75 D could have used better editing.

96A threw me for a while. I had USDA--plants, right?
Oh, THOSE plants! DOH!
Loved this puzzle!  Perfect difficulty for me!
Clever!
Fun!

I liked the themes but run into a few squares that were naticky for me as a non-coffee drinking bit: TRENTA crossing Rowe (and I had TATS wrong) NOE with OSHA, AARP and EAU etc, Some of those answers have appeared before so will eventually fix themselves in my memory.
It was nice to have a Sunday puzzle I could actually solve.

Has anyone enthusiastically pointed out that 77D wasn't greeted with derision as it usually does.  

Could it be us lefties gave it a pass because of the nature of the clue?

This leftie did.  Although I don't think I generally object to having the names of financially rich, morally impoverished people, in the puzzle, if they're going to be there, they should be clued this way.

Cute theme. Solved in well under my average Sunday time.

Re the constructor's question about grid elegance: I generally ignore the look of grid unless I think it might somehow relate to the theme of the puzzle. As the title for this was "Boxed Sets", I did look briefly before beginning my solve to see if there was some box-like pattern to the squares, but there didn't seem to be, so I'm glad I didn't waste more time looking for one.
A very clever puzzle. Thank you to Rich Katz, Will Shortz, and the NYTinmes. I haven’t had this much fun solving a crossword puzzle since Maura Jacobson left “The New Yorker.”

Loved the puzzle! Great theme that made you think a bit without being too out of whack. Well done! 🎉🥳

Noticed s couple of changes to the app? You now have to a knowledge each time you check your answers in the puzzle. I do this several times as I don’t play to keep a streak. 🤷‍♀️ Used to ask only once now each and every time? ☹️ Also once you complete the puzzle it still gives your solve time but no longer shows how much faster the solve is vs your average time. Hope they don’t consider these enhancements 😏.

One more puzzle find, inspired by the coincidence of Will Shortz and Rich Katz. Wondered if there had EVER been an answer in a puzzle with two strings of 'TZ'. 

Well, there  have been three puzzles and the answer in each of those was: 
TZATZIKI
And each of those was between 2010 and 2019. 

....
One of the clues is grammatically incorrect. Thine means yours, not your. Your would be thy

The grid is fine. Not diatracting at all. Loved the theme! My favorite was the most straightforward, TWO BIRDS WITH ONE STONE.

This was SO much fun but certainly not a walk in the park. The first spot where i saw what the themers were doing was 41A. But even then I was fooled for a time into thinking that exact pattern would be followed in all the theme answers. Seems Caitlin had the same issue for a bit. Eventually of course I saw that it wasn’t that simple but when the penny finally dropped I was able to go back and fix the places my assumption didn’t work. 
Thanks Rich Katz, for a very enjoyable challenge. I’m looking forward to seeing your next challenge for us. 
I hope everyone had a Happy 4th yesterday. Mine was joyful, as I joined friends for a local march, with some decorated cars, decorated miniature ponies, happy dogs (a few not so happy about their costumes) and a lot of joyfulness. I was with a very big group of local folks with homemade signs urging everyone to get out and vote and relieve some of the suffering going on in many of our communities and many other countries.

Good, solid Sunday puzzle with a clever theme that was interesting to figure out in each variation. I, too, loved 41-Across the best. Thanks, Rich Katz!
LOVED the theme!! That was so fun and clever. Honestly one of my favorites. My only minor complaint was the cluing on TATS. Thank you for a fun puzzle 🤘
Super fun puzzle, and I was able to set a new PB Sunday time! I let out an audible chuckle every time one of the theme answers clicked into place in my brain. Loved it!!

Started off speedy but really turned into a slog. Enjoyed till I didn't.

Fun till it wasn't. Many of the reveals in the last 20% felt unrewarding.

Theme didn't land as special to me, I got the logic but I feel confident I've seen better versions of similar themes, without the curly braces, maybe.

Have to admit I'm often surprised at what impresses here. I guess I don't get what "amazing" means in this craft as I often find tons of high praise for puzzles which I experience as forgettable or worse.

Sort of hating to be that guy, but I can't resist (as someone who studies and teaches a bit of set theory) ... Sets lack order. The solutions to the big clues rely on the ordering of the members. I was thrown in a way that I would not have been if the clues contained ordered triples instead (ordinarily indicated with angle brackets, like ).

@dutchiris
Maybe you would consider it cheating, but Queen Bee is a lot easier with the spelling bee buddy. I have it bookmarked in my NYT app. It provides the first two letters and word distribution, plus some hints. You can get hints for each word, which for me is too much help.

@Nora
I've used the Buddy, but I do consider it cheating. How can it not be?

@Nora
My method for the Spelling Bee is always the same: just me and the Bee, staring at one another.  I have never used the Bee Buddy, but there are times when I know a word is a word and I can't tell if I'm misspelling it or if it's not in their list of words, and I google it. This comes out about 50/50.  Some solid words don't make their cut and some words I am misspelling.  

A couple of times I have been one word short of Queen Bee, but I just accept that I didn't get it.  I don't think the first two letters would help me, and that's not the way I want to go anyway. There have been times I have had between 40 and 50 words, and still haven't found the pangram, and other times it pops out almost the second I open the puzzle, but I don't want help finding it (or them).

I see it as a workout for my creaky brain, and sometimes more enjoyable than the Crossword.  Maddening, but enjoyable.

@sotto voce

Also I expect there are many, many people like me who don't care AT ALL what a grid looks like.  It has zero to do with my game experience.  I wish it never came up (especially "symmetry") and that constructors instead spent that time and effort on marvelous cluing....

@B

I understand your point. 

Here is the other side, if I may:

I'm not a constructor but I gather, from Mr. Katz's notes, that the grid design is important to them, as yet another facet of the craft and artistry. 

It seems to be an additional point of pride for a constructor, and I can appreciate their striving for it – as Keats has said, "A thing of beauty is a joy forever."

I don’t imagine, however, that it would take away from the time and effort that goes into cluing.
After all, as was done today, beauty is sometimes sacrificed in favor of the game. 
That tells me that the meat of the puzzle comes first. 

My enjoyment of the puzzle, like yours, doesn't depend on the beauty of the grid. But I do find a place for it as a "spectator", though that could be due to my background in Visual Communications which always strives for form and function as one.

@B

Same. I never even notice the grid design. These are crossword puzzles, not visual art. Also, anything that can be created with a limited number of light and dark squares might possibly be creative, but it will never seem beautiful, to me.

@sotto voce

When I go to the art gallery, I don't look at the frames.

@sotto voce

What a little cinematic memory downburst you just made!

In the lower grades, I was a very shy, goody-goody kid, never getting into any trouble.

Sometime between the end of the sixth and the start of the eight grade, I developed a bit of an attitude, and discovered a penchant for smart remarks.  I don't know if it's a defense to say that I thought I was just being funny, but that's what I thought.

Anyway, one day in my eighth grade English class I made some smart remark, trying to get a chuckle, and the teacher, Mrs. Van Pelt (never, ever forget her) looked at me sadly and said, "Francis, you are the most obnoxious student in this class." 

And it just stunned me.  I was still thinking of myself as a shy kid, my self-image not having got caught up with reality of being a bit of wise cracker.

Our neighborhood liaison explained that all three involve stealing, but robbery and burglary designate two ways it can be done. Robbers take something from your person while burglars break into your property. The penalties are different.

@Lynn
Robbers employ violence while thieves and burglars use stealth.  Thus burglary and theft are crimes against property while robbery is a crime against the person with correspondingly more severe penalties. As between theft vs burglary, theft can occur anywhere but burglary involves entering upon enclosed premises. Hence, burglary is the graver offence. These distinctions stretch centuries back in English common law.

Speaking of airfields, I learned many years ago that the human brain enables a person to get used to just about anything.  One of my first jobs in the work force in the early ‘70s was that of a computer programmer with Canada’s Dept. of the Environment.  The building in which our group worked sat no more than 50 yards from a runway at Dorval Airport (now called Montréal-Pierre Elliott Trudeau International Airport) busy with jets taking off every few minutes.  On my first few days at work, the noise was truly deafening.  By the second week on the job, I didn’t even notice.

@Andy did you read the caption?

@Lynn Early forms of digital transmission could send one bit at a time, so speed was measured in bits per second. Yes you can multiply by 8 but bps allowed engineers to talk about what mattered, which was how fast the transmitter could change from one state (symbol)  to another. Later transmission schemes could transmit 4, 8, or more bits per transmitted symbol. (BTW it didn't have to be a power of 2.) Bits per second remained the most convenient common way to describe transmission speed.

Ve haf vase off making you talk =
We have ways of making you talk.

@Francis
I'm afraid your wife is on the trailing edge of home gardening. For decades there has been a move away from the tightly mown flat square of gree with maybe some edging rows of flowers.
Now, in the interests of water conservation, a healthier microecology for both flora and fauna, and a different kind of esthetic, people are creating a more variegated home surround. Stones, gravel, ground cover, and mixed types and groupings of plants provide a more self-sustaining yard to surround a house.
Not that any of this will prevent your wife from being right vis-à-vis you.
